data.title: Manchester United: An Institution Steeped In Legacy, A Business Stuck Mid-Table
data.description:
Thesis

I’m taking a look at Manchester United plc (MANU), the publicly traded company that owns Manchester United Football Club. It’s a massive global sports and entertainment business built around its men’s and women’s teams, its historic Old Trafford stadium, and the money it brings in from sponsorships, TV deals, and game-day sales. The club’s been around since 1878 and commands a global fan base of enormous scale.

Over the past year, MANU is down about 7%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has climbed roughly 14%. Over five years, the stock has more or less gone nowhere against an S&P that’s surged close to 90%.

After going over the latest first quarter results, my research argues that Manchester United is a financially healthier but still fragile club, trading at a steep valuation that leaves very little pitch to recover from getting caught out of position.

Manchester United: Financial & Operational Health Summary

Key Points of Manchester United's first quarter fiscal 2026 results

  • Achieved total revenues of £140.3 million and adjusted EBITDA of £26.9 million, compared to £143.1 million and £23.7 million respectively in the first quarter of fiscal 2025.
  • For Fiscal 2026, the company reiterates its prior guidance of total revenues of £640 million to £660 million and adjusted EBITDA of £180 million to £200 million.

Manchester United’s latest quarterly report offers, once more, a glimpse of a club attempting to remake itself even as it wrestles with the familiar structural pressures that have long shaped the football world. And although the numbers aren’t catastrophic, their commercial revenue did slip a bit, largely because several sponsors either shifted their arrangements or walked away from their partnerships altogether.

Manchester United's first quarter fiscal 2026 commercial results:

  • Commercial revenue for the quarter was £84.2 million, a decrease of £1.1 million, or 1.3%, over the prior year quarter.
  • Sponsorship revenue was £47.0 million, a decrease of £4.8 million, or 9.3%, over the prior year quarter due to changes in our commercial partner mix.
  • Retail, Merchandising, Apparel & Product Licensing revenue was £37.2 million, an increase of £3.7 million, or 11.0%, over the prior year quarter, due to the impact of a full three months’ trading under our new e-commerce model, compared to only one month in the prior year quarter.

Management, as I would have expected, says this is a normal part of doing business, but it also shows that Manchester United can be vulnerable when sponsorship deals expire and that the club needs to keep its worldwide appeal strong to attract big brands.

On the positive side, sales from retail, merchandising, apparel, and product licensing increased. This improvement was helped by Manchester United’s updated online shopping platform, which was fully active for the entire quarter. The numbers indicate that the club has, at least on the operational front, taken some reasonably shrewd steps in its fan-engagement strategy, most likely by improving supporters’ online access and tightening up the range of products on offer. Still, it also raises the question of whether this growth can continue if the broader economy weakens and fans have less money to spend.

While in the sponsorship arena, Manchester extended deals from Canon Medical Systems and Concha y Toro; over in broadcasting, revenue went down this quarter because the men’s team, sorry to say for any fans out there, did not qualify for any UEFA competitions. In the previous quarter, the team played in the UEFA Europa League, which brought in extra broadcast money.

Personally, in case you're unaware, as a lifelong follower of Serie A down south, European qualifications are a huge deal for fans. On the business side, getting into UEFA competitions does a great job of spiking a club’s visibility and, consequently, the broadcast-rights money it pulls in, an uptick that can, quite clearly, reshape this slice of the operation.

So, without the extra cash flow from UEFA competitions, the club ends up leaning more heavily on its Premier League broadcasting money, funds that are dependable, though essentially capped by league-wide rules and therefore not going anywhere fast.

Meanwhile, matchday revenue slipped slightly from last year, and although the number of home Premier League fixtures stayed roughly the same, the drop in domestic cup games is, it seems, the most likely culprit behind the decline. Meanwhile, fan attendance at Old Trafford is still very strong, but how much people are willing to pay could change as ongoing UK inflation may affect how many fans can afford tickets in the future.

Leaner, Sharper, Back in the Black

Operating profit for the quarter was £13.0 million, compared with an operating loss of £7.0 million in the first quarter of fiscal 2025.

The company moved from operating loss to profit largely because last year’s headcount-cutting programs carved the organization into something noticeably leaner. Management, for its part, insists this is all about creating a sustainable platform for reinvesting in the squads. That’s definitely important, and the numbers support it. Employee benefit expenses have now dropped as a result.

Adjusted EBITDA rose for the predictable reasons: once you strip out the non-cash items and the one-off charges, the core operation inevitably looks healthier; yet, at the same time, a few other operating costs crept up slightly, probably reflecting routine maintenance or expenses that move in step with overall activity levels. Depreciation also rose due to recent facility upgrades, and amortization increased because of the value tied to player contracts.

Profit on disposal of intangible assets for the quarter was £45.0 million, an increase of £9.4 million, or 26.4%, from £35.6 million in the prior year quarter.

Profit from selling intangible assets improved, largely because the club managed its player trading more effectively and pulled in stronger proceeds from departures. This approach, though mainly a bookkeeping tactic to stay within tightening regulatory boundaries, also gives the club some room to refresh the squad without crossing compliance lines. Still, the move isn’t exactly risk-free: if the replacements don’t measure up, on-field results can slip, and the whole thing can loop back into the familiar problem of missing out on European revenue.

Net finance swung from income to costs, mostly because unfavorable FX movements hit the club’s unhedged USD borrowings. This sort of currency exposure is, of course, a recurring problem for any club taking on international debt when exchange rates won’t sit still. And industry-wide, Premier League teams are facing the same FX squeeze, a pressure that’s only getting sharper amid broader global economic shifts, including the ongoing changes in U.S. interest rates.

Liquidity, Investment & Competitive Context

Operating cash flow turned into an outflow, mainly because of the timing of working-capital items like deferred revenue and receivables, and property spending rose as the club wrapped up the Carrington training-ground upgrades, a move that, at the very least, signals a concrete investment in infrastructure. And while it’s hardly flashy, this kind of work can improve player development and make the club a more appealing operation overall.

Cash flow highlights:

  • Overall cash and cash equivalents (including the effects of exchange rate movements) decreased by £5.6 million in the quarter to 30 September 2025 compared to the cash position at 30 June 2025.
  • Net cash outflow from operating activities for the quarter was £1.3 million, compared to net cash inflow of £13.3 million in the prior year quarter.
  • Net capital expenditure on property, plant and equipment for the quarter was £17.0 million, an increase of £6.7 million over the prior year quarter.
  • Net capital expenditure on intangible assets for the quarter was £99.7 million, a decrease of £20.5 million over the prior year quarter, primarily due to increased proceeds from player sales in the current year quarter.

Net spending on intangible assets went down because the higher cash coming in from player sales helped offset what was spent on new acquisitions, while financing activities brought in cash through revolver drawdowns, which the company uses seasonally to cover short-term liquidity needs, even though this increases current borrowing levels. Overall liquidity decreased, with cash and cash equivalents dropping because of the quarter’s cash outflows and the impact of currency exchange rates.

In my view, the situation calls for some fairly cautious navigation, since the club has to satisfy both the Premier League’s Profit and Sustainability Rules and UEFA’s Financial Fair Play requirements. Under these standards, the squad-cost ratio effectively caps total spending at 70% of revenues. And with the tighter framework taking full effect this season, it could easily restrict wage growth or transfer moves if revenues fail to recover.

From a competitive lens (really the core of what investing in MANU comes down to), the men's team is currently in sixth place within the Premier League, wedged between Chelsea and Everton. Chelsea's last three matches weren't the best, so it could have a great shot at leaping them to deal with Crystal Palace. Once there, from what I've been seeing, Aston Villa in third place looks unbeatable right now.

The women’s team is in third place in the Super League and made history by qualifying for the league phase of the UEFA Women’s Champions League (congrats to them), which could open up new broadcasting and matchday revenue.

MANU's Valuation

Presently, MANU is priced much higher than most of its industry peers, earning an overall F grade per SA's metrics. Its forward P/E ratio is 31.27, which is almost double the sector median of 16.01, a 95% premium that assumes the company will grow significantly in the future.

In the meantime, Manchester United’s revenue is projected to grow by 4.91%, which edges out the sector median of 3.35%, but only barely. And that thin margin, if anything, suggests the club is still leaning hard on player sales and broadcasting income rather than meaningfully expanding the broader business.

The company’s forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 13.48, far above the sector’s 8.20, a 64% jump.

Its forward price-to-sales ratio is also steep at 3.04, which is 146% higher than the sector median of 1.24.

This swelling confidence does a fine job of mirroring the brand’s undeniable pull, an institution, really, but it also glides past some awkward realities, like uneven performances on the pitch that can trim matchday and sponsorship income. That’s no small concern for a club already carrying about $650 million in debt and leaning on short-term seasonal credit lines just to keep enough cash around.

The forward price-to-book ratio has shot up to 12.57, which is more than 5x higher than the sector average of 1.90. This is that MANU premium again, based on hard-to-measure things like fan loyalty. Historical discounts have improved somewhat, EV/sales is running 12% below its five-year average, but the remaining premium could be a challenge to defend when the trailing profitability numbers are negative.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I rate MANU as a Hold. In its latest results, the company showed small signs of better profitability, but revenue dropped in important areas like sponsorship and broadcasting, which shows the business is still vulnerable to how well the team plays and to broader economic pressures. The stock, as you just saw, is also priced very high in the sector, and Manchester United still carries a lot of debt while dealing with regulatory limits. Even so, I have to admit that if it weren’t for the brand’s history and prestige, there’d be very little keeping me from landing on a harsher rating.

data.title: Holding The Line: Why The BoC May Not Move On Rates For All Of 2026
data.description:
The Bank of Canada held interest rates steady as it warned about modest growth and trade headwinds for Canada’s economy. Andrew Kelvin, Head of Canadian and Global Rates Strategy with TD Securities, says despite those potential headwinds the BoC could keep rates at the current level until 2027.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: The Bank of Canada has held interest rates in the latest decision. And according to our guest today, don't be surprised if they stay on hold all of next year. Joining us now to discuss is Andrew Kelvin. He's head of Canadian and global rates strategy with TD Securities.

And Andrew, welcome to the show.

Andrew Kelvin: Thank you for having me back.

Anthony Okolie: Alright, we always love to have you back. Of course, the Bank of Canada held rates steady at 2.25%. What were the big takeaways for you today?

Andrew Kelvin: So I think there's one lesson that we learned, is that the Bank isn't too alarmed by some of the recent stronger-than-expected Canadian economic data. The Bank recognizes that the Canadian Labour Force Survey is a highly volatile piece of data. And they are not going to let one or two stronger-than-expected months of economic activity or measured economic activity change their view on the broader trajectory for the economy.

Anthony Okolie: Well, OK, let's talk about 2026. What's your outlook for next year? And what are you expecting on interest rates?

Andrew Kelvin: So, for the Canadian economy as a whole, we do think we will see a little bit of-- we'll call it slightly quicker growth in 2026, if we look at things just on a quarter-by-quarter basis. The way we've thought about this is, we believe that the headwinds from trade should have been mostly felt in 2025. They will linger in 2026.

And that's something the Bank of Canada talked a lot about today. They talked about uncertainty from the upcoming USMCA renegotiations, and it's likely to weigh on business investment. They talked about a subdued hiring environment going forward despite the recent very strong jobs numbers.

But at the same time, we think that the headwinds should be a little bit lower than they were in 2025. And at the same time, we do have what looks to be a modestly expansive budget, which was passed fairly recently, which we see boosting growth next year. So I put a bit more fiscal stimulus in the equation next year, have a bit less of a drag from growth. I wind up with the Canadian economy growing around 1 and 1/2% every quarter-- which, in the context of near-zero population growth, is pretty good.

Now, what does this mean for the Bank of Canada? The Bank of Canada is focused on inflation, first and foremost. That is their mandate. They're trying to target 2% inflation.

They said today they believe underlying inflation is about 2 and 1/2%, which is close enough to the 2% target that it would be within a certain level of margin of error. If you stay at 2 and 1/2% forever, they need to do something about it. But for now, they're pretty comfortable with where it is.

We will see the one-off impact on CPI inflation from the removal of the consumer carbon tax roll off in the second quarter next year. At the same time, we have seen a pretty slow Canadian housing market, certainly in terms of price and rent appreciation, over the last year. That puts downward pressure on shelter inflation.

Shelter is about a third of the basket. So, in an environment where shelter price inflation, which lags observed prices-- so that's an important point here. Even if the housing market accelerates next year, it doesn't immediately translate into shelter price inflation. In that sort of a world, that helps keep inflation contained overall and lets the bank focus on growth.

Now, here the concept they really care about is slack. They think that the economy-- very, very simply. If there's slack in the economy, it tends to push inflation lower. If the economy is overheating, if it's running too hot, it tends to push inflation higher.

So they're trying to make it so that there is no slack in the economy, that the supply and demand are balanced-- what they refer to as the output gap being closed. There's still slack in the economy. So, they need to see that above-trend growth to justify not cutting any further.

We think it will materialize. Therefore, we believe the Bank of Canada will stay on hold at 2.25% for all of next year. They do believe that they are at an accommodative interest rate, which is a lower-than-neutral interest rate. When you close your output gap, when you take in all that slack in the economy, the textbook says you should be at your neutral rate.

Now, neutral rates are unobservable. We can have a long argument about where the neutral rate is, and none of us will ever be able to prove ourselves right. Because these things also do drift higher and lower over time.

What really matters, I suppose, is the Bank believes in neutral rates-- 2.75%, 2.25% today. It means they believe we're in a accommodative stance. And once there's no more slack in the economy, at some point in the future, they would expect to lift interest rates.

At TD Securities, we actually believe in the neutral rate to be a little bit higher at 3%. But again, I'm not going to fight too hard in terms between 3% and 2.75% here. All of which is to say, at some point, rates are likely to move higher.

Anthony Okolie: When do you see those rates moving higher? Do you see it in 2026 or much later?

Andrew Kelvin: We think it's January 2027. The markets are pricing in something in the fourth quarter of 2026. I think markets became a little bit overexcited by that last jobs number. But we do believe that the next move will be higher and not lower-- but again, 2027.

Anthony Okolie: And do you think that the Bank of Canada will be focusing on that inflation number, as well as perhaps the job number? And again, we've got the negotiations coming up, as well. How does that play into their-- how does that factor into their rate decision in 2026?

Andrew Kelvin: So in terms of the numbers they'll be watching, they need inflation to remain contained to justify holding rates at these levels. The same way I said they need higher-than-trend growth to hike-- or really, higher trend growth to justify keeping rates at 2.25%-- they also need inflation to be near 2% to justify not moving. Because if inflation starts to drift higher, their mandate says they ought to be moving.

So they'll be looking at the inflation data. And they'll be looking at the GDP data more than the jobs figures. Because the jobs figures, it is just so noisy.

That being said, I'm talking about slack as a thing they care about. The best real-world measure of slack-- that I'm aware of, at least-- is the unemployment rate. So if the unemployment rate keeps moving lower, they'll take that as an indication there's less slack in the economy.

GDP seems a bit more reliable piece of data. In terms of how the USMCA renegotiation plays into things, obviously, if we see another significant material negative change in our trading relationship with the United States, that will have negative impacts on future growth expectations. And that could produce a reaction. The Bank has said in the past that they think they're at the limits of what monetary policy can do to smooth an adjustment. They have said that they believe fiscal policy is better suited to targeting specific sectors of the economy. Because the trade impacts, trade disruptions, they hit every part of the economy differently. Some parts of the economy are very minimally impacted. Some are significantly impacted.

Fiscal policy is better set up to address that. Because monetary policy hits everyone equally. I think what the Bank would like to see is, they'd like to see the uncertainty from the USMCA renegotiation go away once there is a new deal-- if there is a new deal. And at that point, their expectation would be businesses feel more comfortable expanding, investing, hiring.

So that's sort of how that plays in. Uncertainty is bad for growth. It's just very difficult to quantify how bad it is for growth.

Original Post

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Bullish Sentiment Increases Modestly
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, neutral sentiment and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 0.3 percentage points to 44.6%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the second time in five weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 0.1 percentage points to 24.8%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 73rd time in 75 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 0.2 percentage points to 30.6%. Bearish sentiment is below its historical average of 31.0% for the second time in 46 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 0.5 percentage points to 14.0%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the sixth time in 45 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they think the average consumer is faring relative to one year ago.

Here is how they responded:

  • Worse: 56.5%
  • About the same: 24.8%
  • Better: 16.2%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 2.2%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 44.6%, up 0.3 percentage points
  • Neutral: 24.8%, down 0.1 percentage points
  • Bearish: 30.6%, down 0.2 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: PDI: You Were Clearly Warned (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Today, we circle back to the somewhat controversial PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI), or PDI. In our last coverage of this name, we told you it was NOT the time to buy. You were warned about the many reasons, but also we told you where it would be a buy. Briefly, the reasons for this call were numerous. Essentially, the call was made on the trading price at the time in September, relative to the net asset value, or NAV. It was not a buy. We told you that new money should wait for the $16-$18 range. And in recent weeks, we have finally dipped to that level thanks to some big volatility in fixed income and bond markets. Here at about $17.70 we are in the top end of the buying range. Within this range and closer to NAV, we like purchasing the closed-end mutual fund, or CEF, for income. PDI is best held as a small component of a broader set of holdings to generate cash flow. However, it is a busy instrument.

PDI Has A Very Large Number Of Holdings

Folks, you could easily spend countless hours trying to get a complete understanding of all of PDI's holdings. PDI is invested in over 2,000 different things. PDI holds a lot of corporate debt for bonds and has most of its exposure to the consumer cyclical and the real estate sectors.

For the everyday investor, it is challenging to try to follow where all of the money is going. In short, you are paying an asset/management fee (think the so-called expense ratio) to put trust in the fund's management to generate returns and continue paying the juicy monthly income that PDI is known for. That said, here is a look at the recent top 10 holdings, which make up over a quarter of the portfolio.

So we see the sector exposure is concentrated by definition, though we will tell you that within the debt and fixed-income markets, there is diversification of those instruments. PDI makes investments in a number of different fixed-income sectors globally. And that includes things like corporate debt, mortgage- and asset-backed securities, and even government debt. Now, what causes swings in the value of the CEF when it is trading? To a lesser degree big movement in the overall stock market has an impact but largely, PDI can swing with big moves and volatility in rates. When rates move wildly, the value of holdings will fluctuate. This is evidenced on the chart historically. Think back to 2022 when a zero rate environment saw multiple raises. PDI moved in return. You see when there are strong economic changes and interest rates move with gusto, it impacts the value of PDI's assets.

So, you look out longer-term, and you see that PDI's share value has gone down over time, which is just a reality. Typically, slow changes in rates are when PDI performs the best. In general, rate cuts can be considered a positive if they are orderly and not sharp, causing massive swings higher and lower in yields and the curve. While it can be argued the Fed has been orderly and slow in reducing rates, the bond markets have been all over the place. Yields generally have been on a roller coaster, including another recent spike in recent weeks, and PDI has dipped. While that volatility can be unnerving, we like the cash flow here, especially as we trade closer to NAV.

You Buy PDI For Cash Flow

If you look to the consistent payout, and assume this level will be maintained, in theory, the way the math works out you could recoup your entire principal investment through distributions in seven years. Many do not have such patience. The cash flow to investors is indeed reliable. Simply take a look at the distribution history here.

So as we see, there has been well over a decade of reliable monthly distributions, and you will note there have even been a few occasional special payouts.

Returns Of Capital Erode NAV, And Share Value Comes Down

Some of the bearish arguments out there include distribution coverage, noting that the fund does not always cover the distribution, so returns of capital are part of it. Returns of capital can erode the NAV. In recent years, the NAV has held up, largely speaking, even if it has come down some over time. However, as NAV goes, for the most part, so does the trading value of PDI. But we think a bullish argument to note is that this high-yield instrument has never cut its distribution, and that includes through different rate cycles and the pandemic. Yes, returns of capital have been a part of the equation, but the cash flow is there.

There are other bearish arguments centered around earlier buyers that came in around the high $20s. While the income has made up for that, it is a legitimate complaint. However, we continue to think you have to be selective in your timing with the holdings, and we told you to wait for the $16-$18 range for buying. That is where we are now, and you can access the annual distributions of $2.65 per share of the fund, spread out monthly. Since inception, over $42 of payouts have gone out. But timing matters too. We told you at $20 to wait, and now shares are down nearly a full year of distributions. Now you can consider entry and capture the near 15% yield. Sure, since inception and since the top in trading over $30, the principal is down, but you are still earning a strong yield. If you have been a long-term investor and owned PDI since the beginning, you are down from the principal but up from the income.

The NAV has never recovered from the COVID collapse. That event caused chaos in markets and corporate debt. While it did rebound, the rate hiking campaign threw a new wrench into the equation from late 2021 into 2023. Largely speaking the fund stabilized for a few years only to see more recent volatility as yields have been volatile of late. There are a number of reasons why NAV has moved in all this. Simply put, it is a complex bond fund, and those rate movements impact the value of holdings. When the holdings have a lower value, and you sprinkle in some returns of capital over time, you get a lower NAV. And where it gets more technical is in the movement in credit spreads. So you think of leverage here. This is when the fund borrows money to amplify returns, and the value of holdings falls, then it can speed up NAV erosion as management takes action.

PDI's Current NAV

So we like to see investors buy as close to the current NAV as possible. Our $16-$18 range was based on trading at a slight premium to closer to NAV. Or in exceptional circumstances ticking below NAV. But close to NAV or lower is the sweet spot. As of the time of this writing, NAV is just over $17:

So, we are trading at about a 0.68 cent premium here. But the fund took a leg down on the rate cut yesterday and the reaction in the bond market. We think this level is a buy. You have a 4% premium-to-NAV.

Take Home

The premium to NAV has eroded significantly from when we told you not to buy at $20 in September of this year. Now, we have lost nearly a year of distributions in the trading price and have entered our target buying range. At this level and lower we like buying the near 15% yield.

data.title: Intel: Time To Buy Put Options (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Intel stock: Implied volatility underestimates true uncertainties

My last analysis on Intel Corporation (INTC) stock was published on Oct. 30 in an article titled “Intel: 18A Could Change The AI Cost Budget.” The article explained my optimism for its new 18A technology and rated the stock as a hold despite the various headwinds the company faces.

Since then, a few new catalysts have evolved and call for an examination. The rest of this article will examine 3 changes in particular: the potential benefits from exports of advanced chips to China, the changes of INTC’s implied volatilities (IV) in the option market, and also its CEO Lip-Bu Tan's pursuit of deals that potentially benefited himself.

My takeaway from this examination is twofold: A) I see a gap between the IV and the actual uncertainties facing the company, and B) consequently I see a good setup for investors to buy put options. As the use of long options is often associated with a bearish view, this article also adjusts my rating on the stock to Sell.

Let me start with the change of its IV. To wit, the following screenshot (taken from MarketChameleon) shows that the current IV of INTC stock is 52.3, lower than the 52-week average of 54.3. In terms of percentile, it is in the 49% rank, again indicating close to average IV. The current IV of 52.3 is also about 18% below its 20-day HV of 63.5 (historical volatility). Next, I will explain why such moderate IV understates the true uncertainties underlying the business, thus creating a favorable pricing for option buyers.

INTC stock: Large earning uncertainties ahead

To better contextualize the rest of the discussions, let me start with a brief recap of INTC’s most recent earnings report (ER). As summarized in the snapshot below, INTC released ER for FQ3 2025 on Oct. 23. There is certainly good news here. Excluding Altera, INTC beats consensus estimates on both lines in FQ3, as seen. It also raised the Q4 outlook by 3% on encouraging CPU demands across both PCs and servers, largely thanks to (Win11 refresh).

Despite the earnings beat, my view is that Intel's fundamentals still remain challenged and uncertain for a variety of reasons (more on this in the risk section). As a reflection of such uncertainties, the latest EPS consensus for its FQ4 now stands at 8 cents per share (vs. $0.23 in Q3), and GAAP EPS points to a loss of 8 cents per share. The company is scheduled to release the FQ4 ER on Jan. 30, 2026. Analysts’ latest revisions are quite mixed as well, with 14 upward revisions and 9 downward revisions.

For the full fiscal year 2025, INTC’s EPS is projected to be $0.34 at the midpoint, but the range is relatively large. The consensus estimates range from a low of $0.28 to a high of $0.37 among 42 analysts, a variance of almost 1/3. The variance only widens for the next year. For FY 2026, the consensus estimates range from a low of $0.26 to a high of $1.00, a variance of almost fourfold. Also note that the stock’s P/E multiples are quite elevated based on the midpoint estimates, with an FY1 P/E of about 120x and an FY2 P/E of about 68x.

Despite such large uncertainties and high P/E ratios, the stock’s options are priced quite cheaply in my model, as detailed next.

INTC stock: Put option in focus

Due to this gap between its IV and true uncertainties, as mentioned in the foregoing sections, INTC’s option premiums are favorable for buyers in my view. As a specific example, let’s take a closer look at its option chain data (taken from Bank of America). In particular, let’s examine the put option with an expiration date of Feb. 20, 2026, at a strike price of $40. As aforementioned, INTC’s next ER for FQ4 is scheduled on Jan. 30, 2026. Hence, this put option can help existing investors hedge against negative earnings surprises for Q4 (or help investors to express their bullish view for the Q4 ER). The option is also an NTM (near-the-money) option with the current share price of $40.78. The option last traded with a premium of $3.57 per share only. This is less than 9% of INTC’s current share price, hence allowing investors to either hedge their existing position or express their bullish view with a relatively small capital outlay compared to direct share ownership.

Other risks and final thoughts

As mentioned earlier, there are certainly some upward risks at play here. Judging by its FQ3 updates, I see good odds for the CPU demands for both PCs and servers to remain robust into the next year. Also, the Trump administration recently approved the sale of Nvidia’s H200 chips to China with a 25% cut to the United States. President Trump also mentioned that “The Department of Commerce is finalizing the details, and the same approach will apply to AMD, Intel, and other GREAT American Companies.” If this materializes, it will help INTC to gain access to the China market.

However, on the negative side, I continue to see strong fundamental challenges in the near term. The most immediate one is the GM (gross margin) pressure. As seen in the chart below, as of its Q3 ER, the company’s TTM GM is about 33%, a far cry from its 5-year average of 44.67%. Based on the Q3 ER updates, I anticipate the GM to be about 36.5% for FQ4. The Altera divestiture could cause some uncertainties to pressure its GM in the near future. Moreover, the tough competition in Foundry and slow internal adoption of the 18A node (the focus of my last article) are also likely to keep GM pressured in the near future. Additionally, Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan was recently reported to have “used his venture capital background and extensive industry ties to push the company toward deals that also benefitted him personally.” Depending on the final resolution of these deals, they could result in conflict-of-interest violations and add to INTC stock price volatilities.

Finally, investors new to options should note the risks of buying options. My above comments regarding risks were made in terms of the absolute dollar amount exposure. Thanks to the relatively low IV, INTC put options allow investors to use a small amount of capital for hedging or bearish positioning compared to direct share ownership. However, in terms of percentage losses, option buyers face the possibility of a 100% loss of capital in the case of worthless expiration.

To close, the goal for this article is to examine the use of put options for INTC. The examination is mostly triggered by the changes of INTC’s IV, the policy change regarding the export of advanced chips, and also the news report surrounding its CEO's deals. The use of options is part of the strategies we use in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) for both hedging and also leveraging purposes. Under INTC’s current conditions, I believe the current IV represents an underestimate of the actual uncertainties. Combined with the elevated P/E ratios, I see an attractive setup for put option buyers.

data.title: From RNP To RQI: Reviewing Our Best Trade Idea For REIT Funds
data.description:
Relative value is something we stress all the time in a market with a paucity of opportunities. Within the REIT space, we generally avoid CEFs as the leverage makes them unpalatable. Added to that, if you look at the top 10 holdings of most CEFs in this sector, they include the who's who of high multiple REITs. So after all of this poor performance in the REIT sector, you are back to chasing the most expensive names, with leverage, if you buy CEFs. Not our cup of tea. But that doesn't stop us from giving investors heavy alpha generating ideas. Here is one from 6 months back where we squared off Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (RNP) vs Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI).

But if you pick up a CEF, which tacks on leverage, then you are back to square one as far as overall risk is concerned. Investors grappling with this should at least make it a point to buy relative value. That is what RQI represents to us today. Sell RNP and Buy RQI. While we are not buying either, we are putting these as the official ratings as part of a paired trade/switch.

Source: From RNP To RQI: The Best Switch You Can Make Today

We review the REIT landscape, the trade and update our recommendation.

REITs Are Cheap(ish)

This is one of the pictures that has recently got a lot of attention and rightly so.

REITs do look pretty cheap on this metric, if you ignore the axis values and only focus on the fact that the line is at the bottom of the chart. But two things make this a little less than compelling. First, it shows REITs trading at 4.7X EV to EBITDA multiples higher than the broader global markets. Yes, REITs can generally hold higher EV to EBITDA multiples because they can hold more debt. But that is generally no consolation when things go south, as that 6X debt to EBITDA is what often drives something straight to the pink sheets. That is the same reason we have not touched Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE) on the way down. That sword cuts both ways.

Second, the broader markets have never ever looked more expensive. Here is Apple, Inc. (AAPL) trading 14X multiples higher than its pre-COVID-19 high point.

So if that chart made you go out and buy all REITs, you clearly don't know a chart crime when you see one.

From our point of view, yes certain REITs are cheap and we bought some using some protective covered calls. But we would not wade in here screaming "buy everything".

RNP Vs RQI

So we told you to sell RNP and buy RQI. If you did that, you saved yourself 7.5%.

So you tacked on 15% extra annualized alpha for a simple switch between two very similar funds. Where do we stand on those now?

The key insight for those two was that they were identical or near identical funds, trading at very different valuations. In the case of CEFs, the valuation is simply the discount/premium and the Z-scores. You can see below that their spread has rapidly converged since we wrote that article.

We like to look at this in an actual "spread" chart and that shows the move a bit better. The spread was over 12% with RNP trading at about a 7% premium and RQI at a 5% discount. It was a no brainer to switch back then. Today, with just a 2% differential, we are not exactly getting a fat pitch.

Sure, that can compress to zero. It is even possible that this goes in the other direction a bit. But that is not something we would bet on. So, we are declaring the end to the paired trade. We tend to get 4-8 of these a year so we just need to wait patiently for the next one.

Verdict

REITs are a decent asset class and certainly their underperformance has improved relative attractiveness. But outside of a few isolated bargain gems, we are not seeing a compelling setup for the sector. In fact, when we see multiples on Prologis (PLD) or Terreno Realty Corporation (TRNO), you have to immediately think that the sector is not being treated with disdain. Disdain is almost always the norm at secular troughs. So we are picking up a few REITs here and there, but this is not a hand over fist moment. Similarly, RNP and RQI would need to get quite discounted (10% plus to NAV), for us to get interested.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American International Group: Fairly Valued Even If Chubb M&A Rumors Don't Materialize
data.description:
Shares of American International Group (AIG) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 13%. Most of these gains have materialized over the past few days amid reports that Chubb (CB) reached out to explore a bid for AIG. This would be a monumental deal, full of familial intrigue. After all, Chubb CEO Evan Greenberg is the son of Hank Greenberg, AIG’s legendary CEO who ran the firm from 1968 until 2005. I last covered shares of AIG in June, and even with their spike on the CB rumors, the stock is essentially flat since then, justifying my “Hold” rating. With updated financials and M&A rumors swirling, now is a good time to revisit AIG.

Chubb appears to have informally discussed an AIG purchase

News circulated on Wednesday afternoon that Chubb made an informal approach to AIG. In response to these reports, Chubb stated that it made an offer, and while AIG said the two companies are in “regular contact,” it said the company is not for sale. Both statements were notably vague and did not contradict the core of the reporting. It is entirely possible that Chubb has informally discussed with AIG its interest in acquiring the business without making a formal offer. Oftentimes, M&A starts with an informal discussion, as companies will typically want access to private data before making a legally binding offer.

By the same token, a company can be “not for sale” and still receive takeover interest. Indeed, stating that you are not for sale can be a negotiating tactic to solicit a higher bid, and companies can eventually agree to sell themselves after not being for sale. Look no further than the recent developments around Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) to see how sufficient bidding interest can cause a company planning to remain independent to change strategies and agree to sell itself.

Chubb is a willing but disciplined acquirer

Essentially, my view is that Chubb likely has discussed with AIG its interest in a deal, but that any talks were preliminary, with AIG certainly not on the auction block. Chubb has a history of M&A, so this outreach is not in and of itself surprising. In fact, Evan Greenberg acquired Chubb from his insurer, Ace. While Ace was the acquirer, the company kept the Chubb name, given how powerful that brand is globally. While willing to do deals, Chubb has also proven to be price sensitive. For instance, in 2021, Chubb tried to buy the Hartford (HIG). Ultimately, no agreement was reached, and Chubb moved on. I would not be surprised to see a similar outcome here, and Wall Street analysts have already spoken skeptically about a deal occurring.

As I have discussed previously in my coverage of Chubb, I view the company as a best-in-breed insurer with excellent underwriting, thanks in part to a powerful brand that enables it to charge a premium, especially to higher net-worth households. It is also a very global business, one of the few primary insurers with a sizable standalone global P&C operation. With at least $23.50 in earnings, shares trade just under 13x earnings, and this is a primary reason I view a deal as unlikely to be consummated.

AIG's turnaround is largely complete

Over the past few years, AIG has made significant progress streamlining its business and improving results. It has largely exited its life insurance business via its IPO of Corebridge (CRBG) as well as subsequent stake sales. Alongside this, it has greatly improved its underwriting results. From 2008 to 2020, it lost $34 billion on underwriting, but post-COVID its combined ratio has been consistently below the 100% breakeven level.

As I have written, I believe that after the financial crisis, with its brand so tattered, AIG was forced to offer extremely aggressive pricing to maintain business. As a result, it underpriced risk and was left with subpar underwriting returns. As time has passed and the financial crisis has become a distant memory, this pressure faded, and turnover in management led to a renewed focus on improving margins over premium growth. This gradually helped the company improve its pricing and bring underwriting to a very strong place. While not as strong as Chubb, I now view AIG’s underwriting as above average.

This was apparent in the company’s third quarter. Earnings of $2.20 per share blew past estimates by $0.49. Underwriting incomes surged to $793 million from $437 million, primarily due to $311 million lower catastrophe losses. No major hurricanes hit the US mainland this year, which is highly unusual. Hurricanes usually cause some losses during Q3, and I view this year’s results as unsustainably strong. Hurricane activity obviously varies by year, but to some extent, AIG (like most of the industry) over-earned relative to long-term norms. Thanks to lower cat losses, its combined ratio fell from 92.6% to 86.8%. Its accident year combined ratio was stable at 88.3%.

I believe, after years of improvement, underwriting results are now about as good as they will get. As you can see below, premium inflation has been normalizing sharply after its post-COVID rise. With premiums running at 2.2% and overall inflation closer to 3%, it will be difficult to expand margins further. At best, I expect industry underwriting margins to stabilize around current levels, or more likely erode a bit. Given the improving business mix, I believe AIG can sustain current margins, but further margin expansion will be difficult to achieve.

Valuation & regulatory review make a deal unlikely

Given this, I expect AIG to earn about $7.50 next year, leaving shares ~11x earnings. With CB around 13x earnings and the likely need for any deal to be largely equity rather than cash, it would be difficult for Chubb to pay more than the upper-$80s and ensure the deal is sufficiently accretive to shareholders, especially if insurance premium inflation continues to slow. Given Chubb’s proven history of being a price-disciplined buyer, I see price likely being a stumbling block here, as I do not see AIG management as likely to sell below $95, given shares were in the upper $80s just a few months ago. Aside from price, regulatory scrutiny reduces the probability of a deal.

Like Chubb, AIG, even after ~17 years of retrenchment and turnaround since the financial crisis, is still a global insurance company. As you can see below, its commercial business is evenly split between the United States and the rest of the world, and it has personal insurance lines globally as well. Given both companies are significant global players, I would expect any deal to have significant regulatory scrutiny. The combined company would have ~$80 billion in 2026 premiums, and while the insurance industry is highly fragmented, antitrust review would be time-consuming across multiple jurisdictions. While some divestitures would likely be needed, I do believe a deal would get regulatory approval. It may take 12-18 months, and such a long review may be a disincentive to do a deal.

AIG is a suitable "Hold"

Ultimately, the fact Chubb expressed any interest in AIG is further validation of how much this company has improved. Given its size, I do not view other bidders as likely to emerge, and I expect AIG to remain a standalone entity, focused on buying back stock and entrenching its recent underwriting gains. After this jump, shares are back to where they were when I last covered them. With solid underwriting momentum, I believe this is merited, and at ~11x earnings, AIG is not an expensive stock. The macro pressure on insurer margins is likely to limit upside, but M&A speculation will also provide a floor for shares.

As such, I remain shares at a “Hold.” Given I view a deal as being unlikely, I would not buy into this pop, but I view this pop bringing shares more in line with fair value rather than leaving them overvalued. As such, I see no rush to sell. With ongoing buybacks and a 2.2% dividend yield, shares are a suitable "Hold" in portfolios with market-like return potential in my view.

data.title: S&P 500 Cools As AI Earnings Fear Collides With Fed-Driven Optimism
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

The S&P 500 enters a critical phase after closing Wednesday at 6,886 with a 0.67% gain, only to see futures reverse sharply when Oracle (ORCL) warned of rising costs tied to AI infrastructure. The report triggered an 11% drop in the stock after hours and revived concerns that aggressive AI spending cycles may not translate into near-term earnings growth.

The market now sits at an important turning point. Bulls continue to hold technical control, yet the reaction to Oracle’s miss shows how sensitive valuations remain to earnings disappointments. With the Fed signaling a softer policy path, equity sentiment has a cushion, but not enough to fully neutralize the pressure building inside the tech sector’s most expensive names.

Fed softness cushions equity sentiment

The macro backdrop remains central to the index’s tone. The Federal Reserve delivered its third 25 basis point cut of the year and surprised markets with guidance that leaned more dovish than expected. Chair Jerome Powell ruled out additional tightening, emphasized confidence in economic stability, and noted that inflation progress remains intact. Historically, this combination supports risk assets, especially when investors are already positioned for year-end rotation.

The Fed’s approach has helped offset the shock from Oracle’s results, which rattled the broader AI-anchored tech complex. Concerns that multibillion-dollar AI investments may compress margins rather than broaden earnings in the near term added pressure to Nasdaq futures and spilled into S&P 500 sentiment overnight. Even so, monetary conditions and liquidity expectations continue to favor dip-buying behavior into December’s final stretch.

Price structure remains bullish despite futures weakness

On the daily chart, the S&P 500 retains a constructive trend. Price continues to trade above the 20-day EMA at 6,800, a level that has repeatedly triggered responsive buying. The 50-day EMA at 6,728 reinforces the broader support band, and the rising sequence of higher lows remains intact. Momentum has strengthened with RSI climbing back to 60, suggesting renewed accumulation after November’s cooling period.

The index remains pinned below the 6,900 resistance zone, which has acted as the short-term ceiling for almost a week. A clean breakout would likely unlock a move toward the symbolic 7,000 level, an area that carries both technical and psychological weight. Conversely, a fall below the 6,800 shelf would signal a deeper consolidation phase, exposing the 100-day EMA at 6,587.

Intraday behavior shows tightening compression

On the 30-minute chart, the S&P 500 displays a pattern of tight consolidation just below the 6,900 band, followed by a sharp pop before futures reversed. The Supertrend remains in bullish territory, while SAR dots continue to trail beneath price, confirming that short-term momentum is still upward despite overnight softness.

Support rests between 6,845 and 6,860, a zone that has repeatedly launched intraday rebounds. If the index slips below this pocket, momentum traders will likely target 6,800 as the next defense line. Meanwhile, renewed strength into the 6,900 ceiling would likely pull the market back toward breakout conditions, provided the earnings narrative stabilizes.

What comes next

The battle now rests between a dovish Fed and AI-related earnings uncertainty. With liquidity conditions supportive and broad market leadership intact, the larger trend remains upward. But the Oracle shock highlights a potential vulnerability at the top of the index, where valuation sensitivity is rising just as tech spending accelerates. A decisive close above 6,900 clears the path to new highs. A failure to protect 6,800 brings a sentiment-driven retracement into play.

In earlier discussions, we noted that the S&P 500’s strength rested on the durability of its EMA structure and the market’s willingness to buy shallow pullbacks. This dynamic remains intact. The difference now is the added tension from AI-linked earnings volatility, which reinforces the importance of watching how the index reacts around the 6,800–6,900 band.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Energy Stocks And The XLE: Outlook For 2026
data.description:
As markets enter 2026, traditional energy will be a critical economic factor, as oil and gas continue to power the United States and the world.

Crude oil prices have declined in 2025, while natural gas prices have increased. The State Street® Energy Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLE) holds shares of the leading U.S. integrated oil and gas companies and other traditional energy-related companies. Meanwhile, the XLE has outperformed crude oil prices in 2025, and I expect earnings for the traditional energy sector to remain robust in 2026, thereby rating XLE a Hold given its attractive dividend yield in a falling interest rate environment.

Crude oil prices decline in 2025- Natural gas prices rally

Since the end of 2024, NYMEX and Brent crude oil futures prices have experienced double-digit percentage declines.

The monthly continuous chart of NYMEX WTI crude oil futures highlights the 20% decline from $71.72 at the end of 2024 to $57.32 per barrel on December 11, 2025.

The monthly continuous chart of ICE Brent crude oil futures highlights the 18.3% decline from $74.64 at the end of 2024 to $60.95 per barrel on December 11, 2025.

The monthly continuous chart of NYMEX U.S. natural gas futures highlights the 36.6% rise from $3.098 at the end of 2024 to $4.231 per MMBtu on December 11, 2025.

Meanwhile, U.K. natural gas futures prices have declined in 2025.

The monthly continuous chart of ICE U.K. natural gas futures highlights the 42.2% decline from $121.96 at the end of 2024 to $70.52 per 1,000 therms on December 11, 2025.

Dutch Title Transfer Facility (TTF) gas futures are a virtual trading point and the leading marketplace for natural gas in Europe, and have also declined since the end of 2024.

The monthly continuous chart of Dutch TTF natural gas futures highlights the 45.2% decline from 48.889 at the end of 2024 to 26.805 on December 11, 2025.

While worldwide crude oil and European natural gas prices have declined significantly, U.S. natural gas prices have rallied in 2025.

Energy stocks rally- The XLE posts gains and experiences a stock split

While the overall picture for energy commodities has been bearish in 2025, energy companies have profited, and their shares have rallied with the overall stock market. The State Street Energy Select SPDR ETF, the most liquid traditional energy ETF, has moved higher in 2025.

The chart highlights XLE’s 7.8% gain from $42.83 at the end of 2024 to $46.18 per share on December 11, 2025. At $46.18 per share, XLE had over $27.5 billion in assets under management. XLE trades an average of over 29.7 million shares daily and charges a nominal 0.08% management fee. The blended $1.44 dividend equates to a 3.12% yield. XLE experienced a 2:1 stock split on December 5, 2025.

The XLE has an over 40% exposure to the two leading U.S. traditional integrated energy companies

The top holdings of the XLE ETF include:

The chart shows that XLE’s top 10 holdings include a 40.26% exposure to Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), the two leading U.S. integrated energy companies. XLE also owns other leading oil and gas companies, as well as energy-related processing and refining, equipment, and services companies. Meanwhile, oil services have done even better than the XLE in 2025.

The VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) owns shares in the leading services companies, including an over 36% exposure to Schlumberger (SLB), Baker Hughes (BKR), and Halliburton (HAL).

The chart shows the 14.3% rise in the OIH ETF from $271.23 at the end of 2024 to $309.96 per share on December 11. OIH has outperformed XLE in 2025.

Crude oil prices can continue to fall, and earnings for XLE holdings can increase for three compelling reasons

Crude oil prices (CL1:COM, CO1:COM) remain in a bearish trend in late 2025, with the NYMEX futures not far above the critical technical support at the April 2025 low of $55.12 per barrel. Brent futures are also within striking distance of their technical support at the April 2025 low of $58.39 per barrel. Meanwhile, U.S. natural gas (NG1:COM) has experienced a seasonal rally as the withdrawal season, where inventories decline, started in November, and cold temperatures have increased heating demand.

While crude oil prices continue to trend lower, three factors could continue to support the leading U.S. integrated oil and related energy companies held by the XLE ETF in 2026:

  • The U.S. Trump administration’s energy policy supports production and consumption of oil and gas to reduce inflation, establish U.S. energy independence, and increase U.S. oil and gas exports.
  • The U.S. has been cooperating with OPEC+’s leading member, Saudi Arabia, on many global issues. The Trump administration and the Saudis have likely discussed and agreed on a strategy to manage petroleum prices at levels that encourage production and maintain Saudi and U.S. revenues.
  • While the world continues to move toward alternative energy sources, crude oil and natural gas remain the energy commodities that power the world.

These factors could continue to support crude oil prices and earnings for the leading traditional energy companies in 2026. As we have seen in 2025, declining oil prices have not weighed on the XLE and OIH ETFs.

I rate XLE a hold for 2026- The blended yield and P/E ratio are attractive

The most diversified U.S. stock market index, the S&P 500, and the SPY ETF that tracks the index, have a blended dividend yield of around 1.06%. The SPY’s blended P/E ratio is currently around the 30 level.

XLE’s blended yield of over 3% and its P/E ratio of around 16.41 times earnings make the ETF attractive. Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for XLE are mostly positive.

XLE receives the highest possible A+ grades in the liquidity and expenses categories. The modestly bullish price action earns a C in momentum, while the over 3% yield receives a B in the dividend category. The risk of commodities and the crude oil and natural gas markets earns a D- in the risk category, as energy commodities can experience significant price volatility.

I rate the XLE a hold due to the bearish trend in crude oil prices. The dividend yield and U.S. energy policy will likely drive the ETF to deliver positive returns in the coming year.

data.title: American States Water: Still A Hold Rating After 2 Years
data.description:
In this article, I'll be looking to update my thesis on American States Water Company (AWR), a water company that I last covered over 2 years ago. At the time, I considered the company a Hold and likely to go nowhere due to valuation and a lack of upside. Now, 2 years later (and more), that scenario has very clearly materialized, with the S&P 500 (SP500) up about 60% and the company down almost 5% inclusive of dividends.

My thesis and my rating were therefore correct, insofar as this timeframe went, and I was correct in investing in other businesses instead of AWR.

You can find my latest article on AWR here. This poor performance, of course, begs the question of whether the company is now suddenly a Buy or a positive prospect for a conservative investment.

My answer to that would be twofold. First off, we're in a very inflated overall market (my estimate). Secondly, water companies are in need of a correction in valuation to better reflect their risk of capital expenditures, specifically for infrastructure renovations. This is part of the reason why many of these companies have seen declines in their valuations over the past 2 years and why I probably wouldn't forecast any of them above 30x any longer (with some exceptions in some scenarios).

What I want to convey here is that the current market is risk-heavy, and highly valued companies like AWR are definitely part of this risk. On the positive side, the company's once-30x+ P/E is now down to less than 22x average weighted as of the time of writing this article, which, of course, could work as an argument why the company with an A-rating and a decent yield of 2.77% at the time of writing could be a Buy.

So let's see what we have here and what updated estimates tell us here.

American States Water: Lower valuation, still somewhat uncertain upside

Water companies aren't always as stable as we'd like them to be. Part of the reason I was so conservative in the 2023 period was what happened to AWR in 2022. The company made a significant earnings miss at the time, especially towards the end of fiscal '22, due to market volatility. However, at the time regulatory responses from the company's primary regulator, CPUC (in this specific case, the settlement back then did not meet the request for a rate increase or mechanisms that the company wanted to adjust/introduce), had also grown, and thus the company's valuation was in no way “fair” any longer—at least that was how some investors (including me) saw things at the time.

I viewed this as part of a bigger picture as well, where the water utilities across the U.S., depending on their CapEx history, would face increasing headwinds from necessary retrofitting and renovation, coupled with cost and regulatory pressure. In this environment, I saw clear issues in the companies maintaining their premiums.

So, AWR has underperformed significantly. This is despite very strong fundamentals and even very strong EPS growth. Since 2022, the company's EPS has grown over 13-14% on average per year (even if part of that is a reversal from the poor 2022 results). It goes to show you that even when a company performs like this, you can still get very poor returns (negative ones, in this case, even with dividends) if you invest at the wrong valuation.

That's my whole “spiel” as an investor and analyst—I try to find undervalued equities and buy them at that undervaluation. In a high valuation environment, quality and yield will usually not save you—what happened before the dot-com bubble and the GFC is a perfect example. And I believe we're on our way, or are already, into/in the same environment again.

On the positive side, we now have a much clearer and lower valuation for AWR. The key challenge for an investor like me is now finding the new “normalized premium” for these companies, which also takes into consideration what could happen in the event of a market crash or decline (which I believe is coming).

The company's decline of over 20% in a single half-year also dispels quite clearly the notion that water companies cannot be volatile. They certainly can, and are.

Over the years since 2023, many analysts have been positive on the company, for good reasons. The average SA analyst here is at a Buy, while Wall Street and Quant are at a Hold.

In this scenario, I agree more with the latter two than the former. Why is that?

I believe many analysts are underestimating the required premium normalization and are mispricing/not discounting for the potential market downturn we may face, which (while not necessarily involving AWR), will drag down AWR as well, if the 40-year history of crashes is any indication.

Furthermore, while I like the company's military base exposure as a good revenue generator, I don't like the company's exposure to California, given the macroeconomic and political trends currently in the U.S., which can be liberally described as “fluid.”

But positives as well—the company's Q3 '25 results were solid. With a variance/growth of almost 10% due to an increase above all in contracted services, this company's mix is a solid one. AWR's business model has more future safety potential than a water company that only focuses on legacy water services. From that lens, there are a lot of positive trends here. Fundamentals are also beyond solid. The company has plenty of cash on hand and is A/A+ rated. If you invest in AWR, you can (in my view) be certain that nothing fundamental is going to happen to make your invested capital disappear.

Though this may not matter if the capital you invest remains unproductive due to declines for years.

Another positive is that recent rate cases with CPUC went far better than in -22. The company got an okay for its capital infrastructure investment, including some capital investments not expected.

However, all is not “gold” here. The CPUC also fully rejected, once again, a decoupling mechanism and a balancing account. Still, the overall result of the decision, including the decision for Sutter Pointe, can be characterized as “hard but fair.” Given the operating geography of California, it's to be expected.

It also should be mentioned that this company remains a clear dividend king, with consecutive raises of 71 years and counting. This makes it one of the most solid dividend kings on the entire market, with dividends paid since the tail end of the financial depression of the 1930s.

The challenge for the company is neither earnings nor fundamentals, as these are very solid. Instead, the challenge is where we should be valuing the company on both a current and forward basis—and different analysts have different answers to that question.

Valuation for AWR: There's upside, but it's risky

Different answers.

If we look at the 20-year P/E average, which includes almost 8 years of 27x+ P/E valuations, the average comes to 26.78x, or, on a forward basis, a fair value of $105/share, which, from $72/share, would see us having an upside of 20% per year. From that forecast, the prospective PT for the company would be $80/share, and the company would now be a Buy.

That's what I believe to be the bullish thesis for this company,

My argument against this 26-27x P/E valuation is based on two things.

First, the company operates in higher-risk geographies with more challenging regulatory trends. A 27-30x P/E valuation might be something I would accept in a safer environment, but this is not that. It's also a fact that the 26-27x P/E average is discolored somewhat by ZIRP. If we were to remove those years from the equation, the average P/E valuation would very quickly drop to 22-23x P/E, the valuation the company had during and after the GFC. During the long years of 2010 and 2011, the company also traded close to 15x P/E. You might think this to be unrealistic or unheard of for a water business, but this was, in fact, where the company was at the time.

Secondly, the current market valuation and inflation thereof. It's clear to me (and others) that the market in its current state is much higher than it has any right to be. We're not in dot-com territory, but we're approaching it. I have gone to over 40% cash, and while I still invest, I maintain shorter, more agile, and smaller investments than I typically do to preserve cash. This is more important to me than eking out gains in this market, because when the decline comes, I firmly believe it will be significant.

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of $77/share. It's currently at $72/share. This would make the company a Buy. But the risk/reward ratio at the time in 2023 was different, so I'm updating it.

I believe the company should trade at 23-24x P/E, which implies a PT of $88/share for 2027E at current estimates, which have a high likelihood of accuracy. Based on $88/share, I'm lowering my PT to $71/share for a 15% upside, as it's currently closer to 13% per year.

Even with the price at $71/share, I would carefully consider whether this company would be a good choice. There are water company alternatives with more constructive rate environments, better yields, and similar fundamentals. York Water (YORW) has recovered somewhat from the price I purchased it at, but is still at a 15% annualized upside—I consider a 26-27x P/E valid here. Even American Water Works (AWK), a company I cover, can be considered a Buy at a 24-25x P/E forecast range—they are significantly larger than AWR as well, with better earnings accuracy.

All in all, I consider AWR to be the weaker prospect here and give it a Hold. Risks to the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

There are things to like about AWR here, but unfortunately, the company's upside isn't one of them. I believe many of my colleagues are mistaking a “lower valuation” for a “low valuation” in context, and I believe you should really be discounting it more due to market trends. Aside from being overvalued on certain metrics like sales and underperforming sector averages here, the main risks are related to things that AWR does not control. We're talking about challenging regulations, environmental factors, inflation, operating costs, and insurance costs (in California, these are massive due to wildfires, etc.).

Because of this, I do not view the company as properly discounted relative to risk, and other companies are more attractive here.

My updated thesis is as follows.

Thesis

AWR is a dividend king and a water company with an A rating and good levels of overall safety. It has an attractive operating structure and mixes around 80% regulated business with around 20% unregulated. The company has degrees of future-proofing and is a conservative allocator of capital, largely backed by the state of California, which, in my view, comes with a bit of a risk.

At the right and attractive price, this company becomes, as I see it, a “must buy”—but that time is not today. Despite better valuation, I'm lowering my price target (“PT”) and fair value here.

I view AWR as a hold, and I wouldn't touch it above a PT of $71/share. Even $71/share is high, but at least there I could see a conservative upside, which I really can't see at this price, updated for the Q3 '25 period.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 “stars” here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $71/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of December 2025.
